_comment: ROTATION PLANES IN 8 DIMENSIONS
In 3D, rotation happens AROUND AN AXIS.
In 8D, rotation happens IN A PLANE.
There are C(8,2) = 28 independent rotation planes,
one for each pair of coordinate axes: (e₁,e₂), (e₁,e₃), ...
These 28 planes form C(28,2) = 378 pairs. Each pair is
either orthogonal (their axes are disjoint) or non-orthogonal
(they share at least one axis).
The split is 210 : 168 = 5 : 4
168 = |PSL(2,7)| — the symmetry group of the Klein quartic,
the most symmetric Riemann surface at genus 3.
This connects 8D rotation geometry directly to one of
the most beautiful objects in algebraic geometry.

orthogonal.meaning: Planes with no shared coordinate axes (disjoint)

non_orthogonal.meaning: Planes sharing at least one coordinate axis
non_orthogonal.group_theory: |PSL(2,7)| — the projective special linear group over F₇
non_orthogonal.geometry: Automorphism group of the Klein quartic

ratio.music: 4:5 = a major third (one of the most consonant intervals)

_comment: THE KLEIN QUARTIC
Discovered by Felix Klein in 1879. A genus-3 Riemann surface
(a 'donut with 3 holes') that achieves the maximum number of
symmetries for its genus.
The Hurwitz bound: a genus-g surface has at most 84(g-1)
automorphisms. For g=3: 84 × 2 = 168. The Klein quartic
achieves this bound exactly.
It can be tiled by 24 regular heptagons in the hyperbolic
plane — the {7,3} tiling.
